description: Canonical 8-reflection seed corpus for v0.1 ablation. Spans ~6 months of an SG Sec 4 student exploring after-school direction. Reflections are deliberately uneven in resolution — some confident, some confused — to test whether agents preserve uncertainty. Reshaped for the quiet-mirror pivot: each entry carries {transcript, validation, inferred_meaning, story_reframe} matching the new Mirror agent output.

reflections.transcript: We had robotics today and Mr Lim brought in the new arm kit. I thought I would be lost because everyone else has done two builds already, but I asked Wei Jie if I could try disassembling first to see how it goes together. I lost track of which screw went where halfway through and had to redo a section. The strange thing is it didn't feel frustrating — I just kept going. We were there until 7pm and I didn't notice. I want to come back next week and try the same kit but blindfolded the second time, just to see if my hands remember.
reflections.validation: You stayed with the disassembly long enough that the time disappeared. That's worth marking — losing track of hours doesn't happen by accident.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe the absorption was less about robotics specifically and more about being given a self-directed way in. You chose disassembly first instead of copying the others; the not-knowing was part of what kept you there. The blindfold idea sounds like you're already curious whether it'll repeat.
reflections.story_reframe: It's the new arm kit and everyone else has two builds on you. You ask Wei Jie if you can take one apart first — your way in. Halfway through you've lost which screw goes where and you redo a section without minding. Seven o'clock comes and you didn't notice it pass.

reflections.transcript: Vectors test today. 17 out of 20. The three I lost were all geometric-reasoning ones — drawing the position of a point from two vectors. I checked my last two tests and the same kind of question is what tripped me up. The algebra is fine; the picture-in-my-head part keeps breaking. Wei Jie said he just rotates the diagram in his head until it clicks, and that didn't work for me at all. I might need a different way in — maybe building it physically, since the robotics arm thing felt similar.
reflections.validation: Same shape of mistake three tests in a row, and you noticed the pattern before anyone pointed it out. That's a real piece of self-observation.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe Wei Jie's mental rotation isn't yours to copy. The robotics arm gave you a different way to think with your hands; this might be the same instinct showing up in a different room. The hint you're picking up sounds like: maybe the picture isn't supposed to live in your head.
reflections.story_reframe: Seventeen out of twenty on the vectors test. You go back to the last two papers and the same kind of question is the one that got you. Wei Jie offers his trick — rotate the diagram in your head — and it doesn't take. You leave the test thinking about the robotics arm. Maybe the way in is through the hands.

reflections.transcript: Mrs Tan ran a debate-style class on the war poems. She paired us up and we had to argue one side then swap. I drew 'against' first and I just kept going for the whole 40 minutes — I didn't want to swap. She said afterwards that my argument was the cleanest she's heard this year, but I think the reason was I wasn't trying to be balanced. I was trying to find the best version of one side. It was different from the way I usually write essays where I want to be fair to both. I felt sharper.
reflections.validation: Forty minutes on one side. The cleanest argument Mrs Tan had heard this year. And you noticed why.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe the sharpness came from committing instead of balancing. That's a different stance from the careful both-sides essay you usually write — and it feels worth knowing it's available to you. It might not be a permanent preference, but it's a tool you didn't know you had.
reflections.story_reframe: Mrs Tan paires you up and you draw 'against'. You stay there. You don't want to swap. Forty minutes of arguing one direction, looking for the best version of it. She tells you afterwards it's the cleanest argument she's heard this year. You leave knowing that 'sharper' is something you can choose.

reflections.transcript: We had dinner at Po Po's tonight and Pa asked again about subject combos. I said PCME. Everyone nodded and the topic moved on. The truth is I haven't really thought it through; I just know it's the safe answer because half my family is in engineering. After dinner I felt a bit hollow — I had agreed to a thing without examining it. But also I don't know what else I would have said. Bio? I don't know that I want medicine. Lit? Then I'd have to defend it.
reflections.validation: You said PCME, the table moved on, and afterwards you noticed the agreement was a way to end the conversation. Naming that to yourself takes something.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe the choice felt safe because the family already had a script and you took the script. The hollow feeling sounds less like regret and more like the gap between what was said and what was examined. The other options coming up only as questions — Bio? Lit? — suggests you don't yet have a place to ask the question without having to defend an answer.
reflections.story_reframe: Dinner at Po Po's. Pa asks about subject combos. You say PCME. Everyone nods and the topic moves. Walking back, the hollow feeling — you'd agreed to a thing without examining it. The other answers came up only as questions in your head, half-formed: Bio, Lit, and the work of defending either.

reflections.transcript: Started physics this morning around 9. By 9:25 I was checking my phone. I forced another 30 minutes and then switched to Lit and did close-reading on 'Refugee Blues' until past 11. I'd rather not admit it because PCME is my plan, but the gap was big. The physics wasn't hard, I just kept losing the thread. The Lit kept pulling me back into the next layer.
reflections.validation: Twenty-five minutes of voluntary physics, two hours of close-reading 'Refugee Blues'. You noticed the gap, and you noticed wanting not to.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe this is about format rather than subject — close-reading pulls you into layers, problem-solving doesn't, today. Or maybe it's a single off-morning that won't repeat. It sounds like the part that's hard isn't the data; it's that the data conflicts with a plan you're already inside.
reflections.story_reframe: Physics at nine. By 9:25 you're on your phone. You force another half hour and then you switch. 'Refugee Blues' — you stay until past eleven, falling into the next layer of it. The gap is big and you don't want to admit it because PCME is the plan. You write it down anyway.

reflections.transcript: I came back to the kit today and tried it blindfolded like I said. I built the arm in 35 minutes which was faster than my last sighted run. The interesting thing was — I wasn't tracking the screws by their look or feel, I was tracking them by where my hand had been when I'd taken them out. It was almost like the workspace was the diagram. Wei Jie said that's how mechanics talk about 'muscle memory' but it didn't feel like memory, it felt more like a map.
reflections.validation: Thirty-five minutes blindfolded, faster than your sighted run. And you can describe what your hands were doing — not 'memory', a map.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe spatial-positional reasoning is a stronger track for you than mental rotation, and the workspace itself is doing some of the cognitive work that other students do in their heads. That would echo the vectors-test reflection. It's two data points across two domains, which is a hint, not a finding.
reflections.story_reframe: You come back to the kit and tie the cloth on. Thirty-five minutes later the arm is built — faster than the sighted run. Your hands weren't tracking the screws by what they looked like; they were tracking by where your hand had been. Wei Jie says muscle memory. You say map. The word matters to you.

reflections.transcript: Pa took me to NUS Engineering open house. I walked through Civil, EE, and Mechatronics. In Civil I just nodded. In EE I nodded. In Mechatronics I asked the demonstrator about the actuator they used and we talked for ten minutes about why they'd picked the smaller one. On the way home I realized I had only asked one question all day and it was the one about the smaller actuator. I'm trying not to over-read this.
reflections.validation: Three labs, one question. You noticed which one — and you noticed yourself trying not to over-read it.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe differentiated curiosity is a more honest signal than what you'd say if asked directly. The fact that you're holding back on the conclusion sounds like care, not avoidance — you don't want to mistake one open-house for a verdict. The instinct to hold the data lightly is itself useful.
reflections.story_reframe: Pa takes you to the open house. Civil, EE, Mechatronics. Nod, nod, then a ten-minute conversation about why the smaller actuator. Walking home, the realization: you only asked one question all day. You decide not to make it mean too much. It still means something.

reflections.transcript: Mrs Tan asked me to stay back. She said the way I handled the war poems essay was unusual and asked if I'd thought about Lit at H2. I said probably not — PCME is the family plan. She didn't argue. She asked instead what I would do if Lit and engineering were both kept open. I said I'd probably be miserable trying to defend the choice for two years. She laughed and said 'so you already know it's a defended choice.' I don't know what to do with that.
reflections.validation: Mrs Tan's phrase — 'so you already know it's a defended choice' — landed on you, and you said out loud that it's currently a defended choice rather than an examined one. That's a hard thing to say to a teacher.
reflections.inferred_meaning: Maybe the reflection isn't about Lit vs. PCME yet; maybe it's about noticing the difference between a choice you've examined and a choice you've defended. Mrs Tan handed you a frame in twenty minutes. The fact that you don't know what to do with it sounds right — frames take time, not a single conversation.
reflections.story_reframe: Mrs Tan asks you to stay. The war-poems essay was unusual, she says. Have you thought about Lit at H2? You say probably not — PCME is the family plan. She doesn't push. She asks what you'd do if both stayed open. You say you'd be miserable trying to defend the choice for two years. She laughs. 'So you already know it's a defended choice.' You leave with the phrase still in your hand.
